quiz_questions.question_text: Mechanical digestion in the mouth is called:
quiz_questions.explanation: Mastication is the process of chewing, in which the teeth physically break food into smaller pieces to increase surface area for enzymatic digestion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Chemical digestion of starch begins in the:
quiz_questions.explanation: Salivary amylase, secreted by the salivary glands, begins the chemical breakdown of starch into maltose in the oral cavity, making the mouth the first site of carbohydrate digestion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Chief cells in the stomach produce:
quiz_questions.explanation: Chief cells (also called peptic cells) secrete pepsinogen, which is converted to the active protease pepsin by hydrochloric acid in the gastric lumen.

quiz_questions.question_text: Parietal cells in the stomach produce:
quiz_questions.explanation: Parietal cells secrete hydrochloric acid (which creates the acidic environment needed for pepsin activation and bacterial killing) and intrinsic factor (essential for vitamin B12 absorption in the ileum).

quiz_questions.question_text: Bile is produced by the _____ and stored in the _____.
quiz_questions.explanation: The liver continuously produces bile, which is then transported via the hepatic ducts to the gallbladder where it is stored and concentrated until fat enters the duodenum and triggers its release.

quiz_questions.question_text: Trypsin and chymotrypsin are pancreatic enzymes that digest:
quiz_questions.explanation: Trypsin and chymotrypsin are pancreatic proteases secreted in inactive forms (trypsinogen and chymotrypsinogen) that become activated in the duodenum to cleave proteins into smaller peptides.

quiz_questions.question_text: Pancreatic lipase digests fats into:
quiz_questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipase hydrolyzes triglycerides into monoglycerides and free fatty acids, which can then be absorbed by enterocytes in the small intestine.

quiz_questions.question_text: The pyloric sphincter controls the flow of chyme from the:

options.text: Small intestine to the large intestine

quiz_questions.explanation: The pyloric sphincter is a ring of smooth muscle at the distal end of the stomach that regulates the rate of gastric emptying, releasing small amounts of chyme into the duodenum at a time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Nucleases are pancreatic enzymes that digest:

options.text: Nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) into nucleotides

quiz_questions.explanation: Pancreatic nucleases (DNase and RNase) break down DNA and RNA from ingested cells into their component nucleotides, which are then further broken down and absorbed in the small intestine.

quiz_questions.question_text: Brush border enzymes (maltase, sucrase, lactase) are located in the:

options.text: Small intestine on the microvilli of enterocytes

quiz_questions.explanation: Brush border enzymes are membrane-bound enzymes on the microvilli of intestinal epithelial cells that complete the final stage of carbohydrate digestion by splitting disaccharides into absorbable monosaccharides.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is chyme?

options.text: A digestive enzyme produced by the pancreas
A semi-liquid mixture of partially digested food and gastric secretions produced in the stomach
Solid, undigested food in the mouth

quiz_questions.explanation: Chyme is the thick, semi-liquid mass formed when food is mixed with hydrochloric acid and digestive enzymes in the stomach. It is gradually released through the pyloric sphincter into the duodenum for further digestion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does emulsification mean in the context of fat digestion?

options.text: The chemical breakdown of fat into fatty acids by enzymes
The physical breaking of large fat globules into smaller droplets by bile salts to increase surface area for lipase
The absorption of fat into lacteals
The conversion of fat into glucose

quiz_questions.explanation: Emulsification is a mechanical process (not chemical digestion) in which bile salts act as detergents to break large fat globules into tiny droplets, dramatically increasing the surface area available for pancreatic lipase to act upon.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is segmentation?

options.text: The voluntary chewing of food in the mouth
Back-and-forth mixing contractions in the small intestine that blend chyme with digestive juices
The churning motion unique to the stomach
Wave-like contractions that push food forward through the GI tract

quiz_questions.explanation: Segmentation consists of localized, rhythmic contractions that divide and re-mix the chyme in the small intestine, maximizing contact with digestive enzymes and the absorptive surface without significantly propelling it forward.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does cholecystokinin (CCK) do?

options.text: Stimulates gastric acid secretion in the stomach
Stimulates gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme secretion in response to fat and protein in the duodenum

quiz_questions.explanation: CCK is a hormone released by I-cells in the duodenal mucosa when fats and proteins enter the small intestine. It stimulates the gallbladder to contract and release bile and the pancreas to secrete digestive enzymes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is secretin?

options.text: A bile salt produced by the liver
A brush border enzyme on the microvilli
A hormone released in response to acidic chyme in the duodenum that stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion
An enzyme that digests proteins in the stomach

quiz_questions.explanation: Secretin is released by S-cells in the duodenum when acidic chyme arrives from the stomach. It stimulates the pancreas to secrete bicarbonate-rich fluid that neutralizes the acid, protecting the intestinal mucosa and creating an optimal pH for enzymes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does gastrin do?

options.text: Stimulates HCl secretion by parietal cells and increases gastric motility

quiz_questions.explanation: Gastrin is a hormone released by G-cells in the gastric antrum in response to food entering the stomach. It stimulates parietal cells to produce HCl and promotes gastric smooth muscle contractions to enhance churning.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'hydrolysis' mean in the context of digestion?

options.text: The chemical splitting of bonds in macromolecules by the addition of water molecules
The movement of food through the GI tract
The absorption of water in the large intestine
The physical breaking of food into smaller pieces

quiz_questions.explanation: Hydrolysis is the fundamental chemical reaction in digestion where enzymes use water to break covalent bonds in macromolecules (proteins, carbohydrates, fats, nucleic acids), splitting them into their smaller, absorbable subunits.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does lactose intolerance mean?

options.text: A deficiency of the enzyme lactase that prevents the digestion of lactose (milk sugar), causing gas, bloating, and diarrhea
An immune-mediated allergy to milk proteins
An excess production of bile after consuming dairy
An inability to absorb fat from dairy products

quiz_questions.explanation: Lactose intolerance occurs when there is insufficient lactase enzyme on the brush border of enterocytes, leaving undigested lactose in the intestinal lumen where it is fermented by bacteria, producing gas, bloating, cramping, and osmotic diarrhea.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is denaturation in the context of protein digestion?

options.text: The synthesis of new proteins from amino acids
The absorption of peptides across the intestinal wall
The complete hydrolysis of proteins into individual amino acids
The unfolding of a protein's three-dimensional structure by acid or heat, exposing peptide bonds to enzymatic attack

quiz_questions.explanation: Denaturation is a critical first step in protein digestion where the acidic environment of the stomach (pH 1.5-3.5) disrupts hydrogen bonds and other interactions that maintain a protein's shape, unfolding it so that proteases like pepsin can access and cleave internal peptide bonds.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a bolus?

options.text: A lipoprotein particle that transports fat in the lymph
A wave of muscular contraction in the esophagus
A soft, rounded mass of chewed food mixed with saliva that is swallowed from the mouth
The semi-liquid mixture leaving the stomach

quiz_questions.explanation: A food bolus is formed during mastication when the tongue shapes chewed food mixed with saliva into a rounded mass suitable for swallowing. It travels through the pharynx and down the esophagus via peristalsis to the stomach.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient reports that after drinking a glass of milk, she experiences severe bloating, gas, and watery diarrhea within 30 minutes. She has no skin rash or anaphylaxis. Which enzyme deficiency most likely explains her symptoms?
quiz_questions.explanation: The symptoms of gas, bloating, and osmotic diarrhea after dairy consumption without allergic signs (rash, anaphylaxis) are classic for lactase deficiency (lactose intolerance), where undigested lactose is fermented by colonic bacteria.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 50-year-old man with chronic alcohol use presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to his back. Labs show serum lipase elevated to 5 times normal. The elevated lipase indicates damage to which organ?
quiz_questions.explanation: Elevated serum lipase is the most sensitive and specific marker for acute pancreatitis. Lipase is normally confined to the pancreas, and when pancreatic cells are damaged by inflammation (often from alcohol or gallstones), lipase leaks into the bloodstream.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient taking proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) long-term notices she cannot absorb vitamin B12 well. Which cell type's function is being suppressed by the medication, and why does this affect B12 absorption?

options.text: Goblet cells; less mucus means less B12 transport
Chief cells; pepsinogen is needed to release B12 from food
Parietal cells; reduced HCl impairs B12 release from food and reduced intrinsic factor impairs ileal B12 absorption
G cells; less gastrin means less bile production

quiz_questions.explanation: PPIs suppress the proton pumps of parietal cells, reducing both HCl (needed to liberate B12 from food proteins) and intrinsic factor (required for B12 absorption in the ileum). Long-term PPI use can therefore lead to B12 deficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is reviewing a case where a patient had his gallbladder removed (cholecystectomy). The student wonders how this patient will digest fats. Which statement is correct?

options.text: The patient will be completely unable to digest fats
The stomach will compensate by increasing acid production
The pancreas will take over bile production
The liver still produces bile that drains directly into the duodenum, but the patient may have difficulty with large fatty meals

quiz_questions.explanation: After cholecystectomy, bile is still produced by the liver and flows continuously into the duodenum via the common bile duct. However, without the gallbladder to store and concentrate bile, the patient may experience difficulty digesting large amounts of fat at one time.

quiz_questions.question_text: An infant is found to have a genetic mutation that prevents activation of trypsinogen in the duodenum. This mutation most likely involves a deficiency of which enzyme?
quiz_questions.explanation: Enterokinase (enteropeptidase) is the brush border enzyme that converts trypsinogen to active trypsin in the duodenum. Since trypsin then activates the other pancreatic zymogens, a deficiency of enterokinase would impair the activation of the entire cascade of pancreatic proteases.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient eats a meal rich in fat and protein. Within minutes, a hormone is released from the duodenum that causes the gallbladder to contract and the pancreas to secrete enzymes. Which hormone is responsible?
quiz_questions.explanation: CCK is released by I-cells in the duodenal and jejunal mucosa in response to the presence of fats and amino acids. It triggers gallbladder contraction to release bile and stimulates the acinar cells of the pancreas to secrete digestive enzymes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nursing student notices that a tube-fed patient receiving formula directly into the jejunum is not digesting starch well. The student hypothesizes that the formula bypasses the action of which enzymes?

options.text: Salivary amylase (oral) and any early duodenal contact with pancreatic amylase

quiz_questions.explanation: Jejunal tube feeding bypasses the mouth (where salivary amylase begins starch digestion) and the duodenum (where pancreatic amylase continues it). Though pancreatic amylase still reaches the jejunum, the lack of oral initiation may reduce overall starch digestion efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher studying a patient with Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrin-secreting tumor) finds extremely high HCl levels in the stomach. Which of the following consequences would you expect?

options.text: Decreased protein digestion because pepsinogen cannot be activated
Increased carbohydrate digestion in the stomach
Improved fat digestion from enhanced bile secretion
Peptic ulcers in the stomach and duodenum due to excessive acid damaging the mucosal lining

quiz_questions.explanation: In Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, a gastrinoma produces excessive gastrin, leading to massive HCl oversecretion. This overwhelms the mucosal defense mechanisms and causes severe, often multiple peptic ulcers in the stomach and duodenum.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with cystic fibrosis has thick mucus blocking the pancreatic ducts, preventing digestive enzymes from reaching the duodenum. Which macronutrients will be most poorly digested?

options.text: None, because the stomach can compensate for all pancreatic enzymes
Fats, proteins, and carbohydrates, since the pancreas produces lipase, proteases, and amylase

quiz_questions.explanation: Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency in cystic fibrosis blocks the delivery of lipase (fat digestion), trypsin/chymotrypsin (protein digestion), amylase (starch digestion), and nucleases to the duodenum, resulting in maldigestion of all macronutrients, most notably fats.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physician asks a student to explain why acidic chyme entering the duodenum does not damage the intestinal lining. The student correctly explains that which hormone triggers the release of a protective substance?

options.text: Secretin stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion to neutralize the acidic chyme
Gastrin triggers mucus secretion in the duodenum
CCK triggers bile release to neutralize acid
GIP stimulates acid production in the stomach to prevent excess chyme release

quiz_questions.explanation: When acidic chyme enters the duodenum, S-cells release secretin, which stimulates the pancreatic duct cells to secrete bicarbonate-rich fluid. This bicarbonate neutralizes the acid, raising the pH to the near-neutral range needed for intestinal enzymes to function and protecting the duodenal mucosa.
